---
lang: en-US
title: "Deploying the model with a local API"

format:
  html:
    number-sections: true
---


# Introduction

So far, we have trained and evaluated a housing price prediction model, and tracked our experiments with MLFlow. The final step of a machine learning pipeline is to make the model **available to end users**, so that it can produce predictions on new data without requiring anyone to re-run training code.

In this chapter, we build a local **REST API** using [FastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/) that wraps our trained model. A user (or another service) will be able to send a POST request describing a property — floor area, location, number of rooms, etc. — and receive a predicted price in return.

The overall architecture we are aiming for:

```{mermaid}
graph TD
    Client["Client (browser / script)"] -->|HTTP POST /predict| FastAPI["FastAPI (uvicorn)"]
    ModelLoad["loads model once at startup"] --> FastAPI
    FastAPI -->|calls| Model["scikit-learn pipeline (HistGradientBoostingRegressor)"]
    Model -->|predicts| Output["predicted price (€)"]
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Tools used in this chapter**

- [`FastAPI`](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/) — modern, fast web framework for building APIs with Python
- [`uvicorn`](https://www.uvicorn.org/) — ASGI server used to run the FastAPI application locally
:::



::: {.callout-tip title="If you want to fetch back final RF and GB model" collapse="true"}

You can fetch back the final trained models by executing the following code.
The fetched models can then be used to predict any values and evaluate it. 


```python
from joblib import load
import os
import s3fs

# Create filesystem object
S3_ENDPOINT_URL = "https://" + os.environ["AWS_S3_ENDPOINT"]
fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem(client_kwargs={'endpoint_url': S3_ENDPOINT_URL})
FILE_PATH_S3 = "projet-funathon/2026/project1/models/rf_model_final.joblib" 

with fs.open(FILE_PATH_S3, mode="rb") as model:
    rf_model_final = load(model)

FILE_PATH_S3 = "projet-funathon/2026/project1/models/gb_model_final.joblib" 

with fs.open(FILE_PATH_S3, mode="rb") as model:
    gb_model_final = load(model)


```

:::

# Exercice 8 – Deploying a local API

1. We will first deploy a basic fastAPI. FastAPI allows you to define an HTTP endpoint in just a few lines of Python. Here is a minimal working example:

```{.python filename="api.py"}
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def read_root():
    return {"message": "Housing price prediction API is running"}
```

Save this as `api.py` and run it with:

```{.bash filename="terminal"}
uvicorn api:app --reload
```

The `--reload` flag restarts the server automatically whenever you save changes to `api.py`, which is very convenient during development.

If you saved your file into a specific folder, you can access it with the following command : 

```{.bash filename="terminal"}
uvicorn folder.script_name:app --reload
```

For example, you can launch the api with the following code. 
It serves as a fall-back option.   

```{.bash filename="terminal"}
uvicorn intermediate_solutions.5_api:app --reload
```

Then open [http://localhost:8000](http://localhost:8000) in your browser. You should see the JSON response `"Housing price prediction API is running"`.


::: {.callout-tip title="Using the Terminal to test the API directly"}

Without closing the Terminal where the API runs, you can also test the API from the Terminal. 
Open a new Terminal and run a simple `GET` request on the default endpoint with the following `curl` command:

```{.bash filename="terminal"}
curl "http://127.0.0.1:8000"
```

The response should be the default response defined above as a JSON format. 
In the terminal where your API runs, you should also see that it received a GET request and that the response status was "200 OK". 

```{.bash filename="terminal"}
~/work/funathon-project1$ uvicorn intermediate_solutions.5_api:app --reload
INFO:     Will watch for changes in these directories: ['/home/onyxia/work/funathon-project1']
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
INFO:     Started reloader process [42438] using StatReload
INFO:     Started server process [42440]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     127.0.0.1:60728 - "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

:::


If you want to close down the API, go to the Terminal where it has been laucnhed and press <kbd>CTRL</kbd>+<kbd>C</kbd> from your keyboard. 

::: {.callout-note}

In FastAPI, `@app.get(...)`, `@app.post(...)` etc. are **decorators** — they register a function as the handler for a specific HTTP route and method.

Here's how to read them:

```python
@app.post("/predict")
def predict(features: PropertyFeatures):
    ...
    return json_response
```

- `@app.post` means: "when the server receives an **HTTP POST** request..."
- `"/predict"` is the path: "at the URL `/predict`"
- `def predict(...)` is the function that will be called when that happens — it runs, and whatever it returns is sent back as the HTTP response.

So the decorator is a way to tell FastAPI *"wire this function up to this URL + HTTP method"*. Under the hood, FastAPI registers the route and, when a matching request comes in, calls your function automatically.

The HTTP methods map to conventional use cases:

| Decorator | HTTP method | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| `@app.get("/")` | GET | Read / retrieve data. GET requests don't carry a body by convention. |
| `@app.post("/predict")` | POST | Send data, trigger computation. POST requests carry data as input for the API to work by convention. |


In this example, `GET /` just returns a static message — no body needed from the client. `POST /predict` would need to use a POST request because the client needs to **send** a JSON payload (the property features) along with the request. 

:::


2. Building on this first basic API, modify the default endpoint to give back a message indicating what the API does, the model name and version. 


::: {.callout-tip}

```{.python}
@app.get("/")
def show_welcome_page():
    """
    Show welcome page with model name and version.
    """

    return {
        "message": "API to predict housing price in the Paris region",
        "model_name": "Paris_ML",
        "model_version": "0.1",
    }
```

:::

3. Loading the trained model

We load the model **once at startup**, so that it does not need to be reloaded on every request. We retrieve it from MLFlow using the run ID saved in Part 4.

``` python
import mlflow.sklearn

MODEL_URI = "runs:/<mlflow_run_id>/run-relative/path/to/model"  # For example "runs:/04d6f9d4ac4941ebb56a6c9ef2de46c2/model"
MODEL_URI = "models:/<model_name>/<model_version>"  # For example "models:/GB/6"
model = mlflow.sklearn.load_model(MODEL_URI)
```


4. We will now write the prediction endpoint. To do so, we will predict and the loaded model into a `/predict` endpoint.

Update your file `api.py` to insert this new endpoint. 
Don't forget to transform data so that it matches the data types expected from the model. 
Input should have a similar format to `X_test`. 

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: See the solution
#| code-overflow: scroll
#| eval: false

import pandas as pd
import mlflow.sklearn
from fastapi import FastAPI

# ── App initialization ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

app = FastAPI(
    title="Housing Price Prediction API",
    description="Predicts the price and price per m² of a French residential property.",
    version="1.0.0",
)


# ── Model loading (done once at startup) ────────────────────────────────────

MODEL_URI = "models:/GB/6"
model = mlflow.sklearn.load_model(MODEL_URI)


# ── Endpoints ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

@app.get("/")
def show_welcome_page(MODEL_URI=MODEL_URI):
    """
    Show welcome page with model name and version.
    """

    return {
        "message": "API to predict housing price in the Paris region",
        "model_name": "Paris_ML",
        "model_version": MODEL_URI,
    }

@app.post("/predict")
def predict(prop_features: dict):
    """
    Accepts a JSON description of a property and returns the predicted price per m²
    and the estimated total price.
    """

    prop_features_df = pd.DataFrame([prop_features])

    # Turning prop_type to Categorical data
    prop_features_df["prop_type"] = pd.Categorical(
        prop_features_df["prop_type"],
        categories=["1", "2"],
        ordered=False
    ).rename_categories({"1": "House", "2": "Flat"})

    # Turning trans_date to datetime format
    prop_features_df["trans_date"] = pd.to_datetime(prop_features_df["trans_date"], format="%d/%m/%Y")

    # Storing floor area
    farea = prop_features["farea"]

    # Run the prediction
    predicted_price_sqm = model.predict(prop_features_df)[0]
    predicted_price = predicted_price_sqm * farea

    return {
        "predicted_price_sqm": round(float(predicted_price_sqm), 0),
        "predicted_price": round(float(predicted_price), 0),
        "farea": farea,
    }

```



5. We will check that the API runs locally. Predict the price of a property with the following characteristics using python's `requests` : 
- a flat (prop_type: 2):
  - a floor area of 65m²;
  - one shower;
  - one WC;
  - 3 main rooms : 2 sleeping rooms, 1 small kitchen (it's Paris); 
  - on the third floor; 
  - with one terrace, one attic and one basement;
- the building: 
  - is located at 85, Rue Manin 75019 Paris (look up the latitude and longitude);
  - has been built in 1870;
  - has 6 floors;
  - has an elevator;
  - has stairs. 


To avoid typing everything, here is the list of variables.
Don't forget to see how the variables are encoded in the [dedicated section](intro_data.qmd) ! 


``` python
{
    "farea": 10,
    "trans_date": "01/02/2023",
    "trans_year": 2023,
    "trans_month": 2,
    "prop_type": 2,
    "prop_year_harm_10": 1850,
    "prop_loc_x": 2.1,
    "prop_loc_y": 48.8, 
    "has_cheating": 0, 
    "has_elec": 0, 
    "has_elevator": 1,
    "has_gas": 0, 
    "has_mdrainage": 2, 
    "has_rchute": 0,
    "has_water": 0, 
    "n_floors": 6,
    "n_bath": 0,
    "n_eatr": 0,
    "n_kit8": 1,
    "n_kit9": 0,
    "n_ancrooms": 0,
    "n_attic": 1,
    "n_basmt": 1,
    "n_garage": 0, 
    "n_pool": 0, 
    "n_mrooms": 3,
    "n_otherannex": 0,
    "n_rooms": 4,
    "n_show": 1,
    "n_sink": 1,
    "n_slr": 2,
    "n_terrace": 1,
    "n_washr": 1,
    "n_wc": 1,
    "nth_floor": 3,
    "s_land_agri": 0, 
    "s_land_artif": 0, 
    "s_land_nat": 0, 
    "stair": 1 
  }
```

You can now query your API with python's `request`.



```{python}
#| eval: false
import requests

prop_features = {
  "farea": 65,
  "trans_date": "01/02/2023",
  "trans_year": 2023,
  "trans_month": 2,
  "prop_type": 2,
  "prop_year_harm_10": 1870,
  "prop_loc_x": 2.3845,
  "prop_loc_y": 48.8830, 
  "has_cheating": 0, 
  "has_elec": 2, 
  "has_elevator": 2,
  "has_gas": 2, 
  "has_mdrainage": 2, 
  "has_rchute": 0,
  "has_water": 2, 
  "n_floors": 6,
  "n_bath": 0,
  "n_eatr": 0,
  "n_kit8": 1,
  "n_kit9": 0,
  "n_ancrooms": 0,
  "n_attic": 1,
  "n_basmt": 1,
  "n_garage": 0, 
  "n_pool": 0, 
  "n_mrooms": 3,
  "n_otherannex": 0,
  "n_rooms": 4,
  "n_show": 1,
  "n_sink": 1,
  "n_slr": 2,
  "n_terrace": 1,
  "n_washr": 1,
  "n_wc": 1,
  "nth_floor": 3,
  "s_land_agri": 0, 
  "s_land_artif": 0, 
  "s_land_nat": 0, 
  "stair": 2 
  }

response = requests.post("http://localhost:8000/predict", json=prop_features)
print(response.json())
```

Expected output:

```{.json}
{'predicted_price_sqm': 2081.0, 'predicted_price': 135273.0, 'farea': 65}
```



# Summary

Congrats 🎉! In this section, we have:

- Built a **FastAPI** application that wraps our trained scikit-learn model,
- Exposed a `/predict` endpoint that returns a predicted `price_sqm` and total `predicted_price`,
- Tested the API locally `requests`.

The next natural step — beyond the scope of this tutorial — would be to containerize this API with Docker and deploy it on a cloud platform so it becomes accessible to external users.
